preamble: The shift from the alveolar stop [d] to the lateral approximant [l] is a well-documented historical phenomenon. It is most famously associated with the 'Sabine L' in early Italic dialects, where words with an original [d] were borrowed into Classical Latin with an [l].

examples.note: Sabine L: PIE *dakru- 'tear' → Latin lacrima via Sabine dialect influence replacing initial d- with l-.
PIE *dn̥gʷhu- 'tongue': Oscan form with d- replaced by l- in Latin under Sabine influence.
Greek initial O-dy- adapted as Uli- in Latin, possibly through Etruscan mediation.
